---
title: "Appendix D: Portfolio Theory"
subtitle: "Mean-Variance Frontier, CAPM, APT, and Robust Estimation"
---

::: {.copyright-notice}
**Copyright Notice.** Planned for publication in 2026 by R. Douglas Martin, Thomas K. Philips, Bernd Scherer, and Kirk Li. All rights reserved. © Copyright 2025.
:::

::: {.pdf-callout}
::: {.pdf-icon}
&#128196;
:::
::: {.pdf-text}
**Full appendix available as PDF.**
[Download Appendix D — Portfolio Theory](Appendix%20D%20Portfolio%20Theory.pdf){target="_blank"}
:::
:::

## Overview

This appendix derives the mathematical results underlying classical portfolio theory: the minimum-variance frontier, the Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM), the zero-beta CAPM, and the Arbitrage Pricing Theory (APT). It also provides technical details on the robust estimation methods used in the main text.

---

## D.1 — Minimum-Variance Frontier Mathematics

::: {.section-list}
**Topics covered:**

- Derivation of the minimum-variance weight vector $\mathbf{w}_{\mathrm{mv}}$
- Closed-form solution using Lagrange multipliers (two-fund separation)
- The constants $a, b, c, d$ and their geometric meaning
- Positivity of $d$ via the Cauchy-Schwarz inequality
- Global minimum variance (GMV) portfolio: mean $\mu_{\mathrm{gmv}} = a/c$ and variance $\sigma^2_{\mathrm{gmv}} = 1/c$
:::

The minimum-variance portfolio with target return $\mu_P$, subject to full investment $\mathbf{w}^\prime \mathbf{1} = 1$ and return constraint $\mathbf{w}^\prime \boldsymbol{\mu} = \mu_P$, has weight vector

$$
\mathbf{w}_{\mathrm{mv}} = \mathbf{w}_1 + \mu_P\,\mathbf{w}_2,
$$

where $\mathbf{w}_1$ and $\mathbf{w}_2$ are two fixed portfolios (the **two-fund separation** result). The efficient frontier is the upper branch of the resulting parabola in $(\sigma_P, \mu_P)$ space.

The scalar $d = bc - a^2 > 0$ (with equality only when all expected returns are identical, which never arises in practice) follows from the Cauchy-Schwarz inequality applied to $\mathbf{C}^{-1/2}\boldsymbol{\mu}$ and $\mathbf{C}^{-1/2}\mathbf{1}$.

---

## D.2 — The Tangent Portfolio is the Market Portfolio Under the CAPM

::: {.section-list}
**Topics covered:**

- Definition of the tangent portfolio: maximum Sharpe ratio portfolio
- Derivation that the tangent portfolio equals the market portfolio under CAPM assumptions
- Implications: all investors hold a combination of the risk-free asset and the market portfolio
:::

---

## D.3 — Proof of the CAPM

::: {.section-list}
**Topics covered:**

- CAPM assumptions: investors maximize mean-variance utility, homogeneous expectations, risk-free asset
- Derivation of the Security Market Line (SML): $\mu_i - r_f = \beta_i (\mu_M - r_f)$
- Beta as the scaled covariance: $\beta_i = \mathrm{cov}(R_i, R_M)/\sigma_M^2$
:::

The **Capital Asset Pricing Model** states that in equilibrium, every asset's expected excess return is proportional to its market beta:

$$
\mu_i - r_f = \beta_i \left(\mu_M - r_f\right), \quad \beta_i = \frac{\mathrm{cov}(R_i, R_M)}{\sigma_M^2}.
$$

The CAPM is derived by exploiting the fact that the market portfolio must lie on the efficient frontier, and using the first-order conditions of the investor's mean-variance optimization problem.

---

## D.4 — Zero-Beta CAPM Derivation

::: {.section-list}
**Topics covered:**

- The zero-beta portfolio $Z(P)$ of a frontier portfolio $P$: the unique frontier portfolio uncorrelated with $P$
- Black's (1972) zero-beta CAPM (no risk-free asset): $\mu_i = \mu_{Z(M)} + \beta_i (\mu_M - \mu_{Z(M)})$
- Geometric interpretation on the minimum-variance frontier
:::

::: {#fig-zero-beta layout-ncol=1}
![Zero-beta portfolio on the minimum-variance frontier. For any frontier portfolio $P$ (here the market portfolio $M$), its zero-beta counterpart $Z(M)$ is the frontier portfolio with zero covariance with $M$. The zero-beta CAPM replaces the risk-free rate with the expected return of $Z(M)$, making it applicable when no risk-free asset exists.](4C__Users_tkpme_Dropbox_BookPCRA_Springer_Produ___D_Portfolio_Theory_Plots_zeroBetaPort_graph.PNG){fig-alt="Zero beta portfolio on the mean-variance frontier"}

Zero-beta CAPM: $Z(M)$ is the frontier portfolio uncorrelated with the market portfolio $M$.
:::

---

## D.5 — Zero-Covariance Frontier Portfolios

::: {.section-list}
**Topics covered:**

- Characterization of all pairs of zero-covariance frontier portfolios
- The zero-covariance map on the parabolic frontier
:::

---

## D.6 — Proof of the APT

::: {.section-list}
**Topics covered:**

- Ross (1976) Arbitrage Pricing Theory: factor model structure and no-arbitrage pricing
- Factor risk premia and factor betas
- Relationship between APT and CAPM
:::

The **Arbitrage Pricing Theory** assumes returns follow a $K$-factor linear model:

$$
R_i = \alpha_i + \sum_{k=1}^{K} \beta_{ik} F_k + \varepsilon_i.
$$

Under a no-arbitrage condition, the expected return satisfies

$$
\mu_i \approx r_f + \sum_{k=1}^{K} \beta_{ik}\, \lambda_k,
$$

where $\lambda_k$ is the risk premium of factor $k$. The APT requires no distributional assumptions on returns beyond the factor structure.

---

## D.7 — Robust Estimation Details

::: {.section-list}
**Topics covered:**

- mOpt $\psi$ function formula: the bounded score function used in MM-regression
- Technical details supporting the robust estimators discussed in the main text
:::

::: {.callout-note}
## Reading Guide

Section D.1 is the mathematical foundation for all portfolio construction results in PCRA. Readers with a strong linear algebra background (covered in Appendix A) will find the derivations in D.1–D.4 straightforward. Section D.6 (APT) is self-contained and can be read independently.
:::
